questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents after a fall while getting out of bed in the morning. She felt 'dizzy' and her vision went black. She recovered quickly lying down. Medications: ramipril 10 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. BP supine 140/85, standing (1 min) 105/70, heart rate 78. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The postural drop in systolic BP >30 mmHg with symptoms is diagnostic of orthostatic hypotension, commonly caused by antihypertensive medications in elderly patients. Vasovagal syncope typically has a prodrome and is not solely postural. Postprandial hypotension occurs after meals. Carotid sinus hypersensitivity requires specific triggers. Cardiac arrhythmia would be unlikely with normal sinus rhythm at 78 bpm.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with a history of falls, often when turning his head to look behind him. He describes brief loss of consciousness lasting seconds. No palpitations. ECG shows sinus rhythm. Carotid sinus massage (under monitoring) reproduces symptoms with >3 second asystole. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of head turning triggering syncope and reproduction of symptoms with asystole on massage is diagnostic of carotid sinus hypersensitivity. Vasovagal syncope is not head-turn related. Sick sinus syndrome requires ECG evidence of bradyarrhythmia. Orthostatic hypotension has postural drop. Seizures have post-ictal confusion and are not triggered by neck movement.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman presents with recurrent falls over the past year. She has a wide-based gait, difficulty turning, and reports urinary urgency. She has no tremor or rigidity. Cognitive assessment shows mild impairment (MOCA 22/30). MRI brain shows ventriculomegaly without cortical atrophy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of gait apraxia (falls), urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline with ventriculomegaly on imaging is classic for normal pressure hydrocephalus. Alzheimer's and vascular dementia lack gait apraxia and ventriculomegaly. Parkinson's disease would have tremor/rigidity. Cervical myelopathy would have upper motor neuron signs in legs and neck pain.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man collapses while mowing the lawn. He had chest tightness but no palpitations. He is known to have hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia. On examination, he has a harsh mid-systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids. Carotid upstroke is delayed. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Exertional syncope with a harsh systolic murmur at the aortic area radiating to carotids and delayed carotid upstroke is classic for severe aortic stenosis. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy has a murmur that increases with Valsalva. Pulmonary embolism typically presents with dyspnoea. Myocardial infarction usually has chest pain at rest. Vasovagal syncope is not exertional without anxiety.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with Parkinson's disease presents after a fall. Her usual medications include co-beneldopa. She reports that she sometimes feels her feet are 'glued to the floor' when trying to walk through a doorway. She is otherwise stable. What is the single most likely cause of her fall?
questions.explanation: The description of feet feeling 'glued to the floor' when initiating gait or turning is characteristic of freezing of gait, a common dopa-responsive symptom in Parkinson's disease. Orthostatic hypotension would have light-headedness on standing. Dyskinesia is involuntary movements, not gait freezing. Postural instability occurs with retropulsion. Tremor does not cause falls directly.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman presents to the falls clinic with three falls in the past six months. She describes dizziness upon standing but no loss of consciousness. Her lying blood pressure is 148/84 mmHg. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
options: Active stand test (lying and standing blood pressure)
questions.explanation: The history suggests orthostatic hypotension; active stand test is the first-line diagnostic test per NICE. Tilt-table test is reserved if the diagnosis remains uncertain after active stand test.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a witnessed collapse. He was unconscious for 10 seconds and then recovered. ECG shows bifascicular block (right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block). What is the single most appropriate next investigation to identify the cause of his fall?
questions.explanation: In syncope with bifascicular block, paroxysmal complete heart block is a concern; prolonged ECG monitoring (e.g., 24-hour Holter) is indicated to detect intermittent high-grade block. Troponin and echo are for ischaemic cause, but history is more suggestive of arrhythmia.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on warfarin sustains a mechanical fall from standing height. She did not lose consciousness, but has a scalp haematoma. In the emergency department, she is alert and orientated. What is the single most appropriate investigation to rule out a significant intracranial injury?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend urgent CT head (within 1 hour) for patients on anticoagulation with head injury, even with no loss of consciousness, as they are at high risk of intracranial haemorrhage. Coagulation screen is important but does not rule out injury; CT head is the primary investigation.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man is referred for recurrent unexplained falls. He has no history of syncope but reports feeling lightheaded on turning his head. There is no cardiovascular disease. What is the single most appropriate next diagnostic test?
questions.explanation: In older adults with unexplained falls and symptoms triggered by head turning, carotid sinus hypersensitivity is suspected. Carotid sinus massage is the diagnostic test of choice (if no contraindications) per NICE.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man presents with a two-year history of slow walking and two falls. His family reports he has become forgetful and occasionally loses bladder control. On examination, his gait is short-stepped and broad-based. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of gait instability, cognitive decline, and urinary incontinence suggests normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH). MRI brain (or CT) can show ventricular enlargement disproportionate to sulcal atrophy, which is characteristic. Lumbar puncture may be used to assess response to CSF drainage but the initial diagnostic imaging is MRI.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman presents after a fall. She has a history of hypertension on ramipril 5mg daily. BP 130/80 lying, 100/60 standing. She feels dizzy on standing. Investigations exclude other causes. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Advise compression stockings and increased fluid/salt intake
questions.explanation: Non-pharmacological measures (compression stockings, increased fluid/salt) are first-line for orthostatic hypotension per NICE. Stopping ramipril may be considered later, but it is not first-line as it may be required for hypertension control. Fludrocortisone and midodrine are second-line if non-pharmacological fails.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with Parkinson's disease has had three falls in the past month. He is on co-beneldopa 125mg four times daily. He reports no postural hypotension or dyskinesias. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Refer for multidisciplinary falls clinic including physiotherapy and occupational therapy
questions.explanation: Falls in Parkinson's are often due to postural instability, which is dopamine-resistant. First-line management is non-pharmacological: physiotherapy (balance training) and occupational therapy (home assessment). Increasing levodopa or adding amantadine may help motor fluctuations but not falls; anticholinergics can worsen cognition and falls risk; DBS is for refractory motor complications, not first-line.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man taking rivaroxaban 20mg daily for atrial fibrillation falls and sustains a head injury. CT head shows an acute subdural haematoma. He is GCS 15 and haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Stop rivaroxaban and administer prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)
Stop rivaroxaban and administer fresh frozen plasma
Stop rivaroxaban and administer vitamin K
questions.explanation: For life-threatening bleeding on a direct oral anticoagulant like rivaroxaban, reversal with prothrombin complex concentrate (or andexanet alfa if available) is indicated. Fresh frozen plasma is used for warfarin reversal; vitamin K does not reverse rivaroxaban; tranexamic acid is not an anticoagulant reversal agent. Stopping alone is insufficient for intracranial haemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman presents with a Colles' fracture after a fall from standing height. She has no previous fractures. DXA scan reports a T-score of -2.8 at the hip. She has normal renal function and no contraindications. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start alendronic acid 70mg weekly with calcium and vitamin D supplementation
questions.explanation: A fragility fracture with T-score ≤ -2.5 indicates osteoporosis. First-line pharmacological treatment per NICE is a bisphosphonate, typically alendronic acid, with calcium and vitamin D. Teriparatide is second-line; HRT is not first-line for older women; strontium ranelate is rarely used. Lifestyle alone is insufficient given the fracture.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old woman with Alzheimer's dementia and hypertension presents after a fall. She is on donepezil 10mg daily, amlodipine 5mg daily, and temazepam 10mg at night for insomnia. She has no postural hypotension. What is the most appropriate management to reduce falls risk?
options: Refer for physiotherapy and balance training
questions.explanation: Benzodiazepines (temazepam) significantly increase falls risk due to sedation and impaired balance. Deprescribing psychotropic medications is a key intervention for falls prevention. Donepezil may cause bradycardia but is less strongly associated with falls; amlodipine is not a major fall risk; physiotherapy and vitamin D are adjunctive but not the single most appropriate first step.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after a fall at home. She is uninjured, can mobilise, and has no head strike. Observations are normal. She has a history of hypertension and takes ramipril. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check lying and standing blood pressure
Start vitamin D and calcium supplementation
Discharge with advice to use a walking stick
questions.explanation: In an older adult presenting after a fall, the immediate priority is to assess for postural hypotension, a common cause. Lying and standing BP measurements guide further management. Referral and supplementation are important but not urgent; CT head is not indicated without head injury or symptoms.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on warfarin is found on the floor after a mechanical fall. He has a 5 cm scalp laceration, GCS 14 (E3 V5 M6), and is confused. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: Given the combination of anticoagulation, head injury, and reduced GCS, there is high risk of intracranial haemorrhage. The most urgent step is a CT head to rule out bleeding. INR is important but not the immediate action; suturing and vitamin K can wait until after imaging.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with mild dementia presents to the GP with recurrent falls over the past 3 months. She has no acute injury. Her medications include amitriptyline 25 mg nocte for neuropathic pain and bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily for hypertension. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a medication review and consider stopping amitriptyline
Refer for a home hazard assessment
Refer to a geriatrician for falls clinic
questions.explanation: Medication review is a key first step in multifactorial falls assessment, especially as amitriptyline has strong anticholinergic and sedative effects that increase fall risk. Stopping or reducing it is likely more impactful initially than other assessments or treatments.

questions.stem: An 85-year-old man is in hospital with a right neck of femur fracture awaiting surgery. On the morning of surgery, he develops sudden-onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath. Respiratory rate 28, SpO2 88% on room air, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give oxygen via a non-rebreather mask
questions.explanation: This patient is acutely hypoxic (SpO2 88%). Although pulmonary embolism is likely, the immediate priority is to correct hypoxia. Oxygen therapy should be given first before diagnostic tests or definitive therapy. ECG and CTPA can follow once oxygen is administered.

questions.stem: An 88-year-old nursing home resident with advanced dementia and recurrent falls is brought to the emergency department with fever, confusion, and hypotension (BP 80/50 mmHg). Heart rate 110 bpm, respiratory rate 24, temperature 38.9°C. Capillary refill time 4 seconds. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Perform a CT head to rule out intracranial pathology
Start intravenous fluids as a bolus
Take blood cultures and a urine sample
questions.explanation: This patient has septic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, fever, confusion). The immediate priority is resuscitation with intravenous fluids to restore perfusion, as per the sepsis 6 bundle. Antibiotics and cultures are crucial but should follow or be simultaneous with fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents after a fall. She bumped her head but did not lose consciousness. She has a GCS of 15 and is otherwise well. Which single clinical finding would indicate the need for an urgent CT head?
options: Pain at the site of impact
questions.explanation: In patients on anticoagulation with head injury, amnesia for the event (even without loss of consciousness) is a red flag for intracranial haemorrhage per NICE guidelines. Vomiting is also a red flag but only if more than once; once is borderline. Amnesia is the most definitive here.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man is found on the floor of his flat after a fall. He is unable to get up and may have been there for over 12 hours. On examination, his hip is tender but he can move all limbs. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests rhabdomyolysis requiring immediate intravenous fluids?
questions.explanation: Dark brown urine is pathognomonic of myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis. Early aggressive IV fluids are needed to prevent acute kidney injury. While other signs may be present, dark urine is the most specific.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of falls is brought to ED after being found confused at home. He has a GCS of 13 and is drowsy. On examination, his left pupil is dilated and sluggish. Which single finding is most concerning for raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: A unilateral dilated and sluggish pupil indicates third nerve compression from uncal herniation, a surgical emergency. Papilloedema is a later sign. Right sided weakness would suggest contralateral lesion but does not itself indicate herniation.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman underwent hemiarthroplasty for a fractured neck of femur three days ago. She now develops sudden onset dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests pulmonary embolism as the cause?
questions.explanation: Unilateral calf swelling suggests deep vein thrombosis, which is a common source of pulmonary embolism. In a postoperative patient with classic symptoms, this finding significantly increases the probability of PE.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on gliclazide is brought to the emergency department after a fall. His wife reports he suddenly became confused and had weakness of his right arm. On examination, he is sweaty and confused. His GCS is 14. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests that this is not a stroke?
questions.explanation: Sweating is a typical adrenergic symptom of hypoglycaemia. In a diabetic on sulfonylurea with focal neurological signs, it should raise suspicion for hypoglycaemic hemiplegia. Immediate glucose check is warranted.
